FT_IC1.evidence: Study explicitly uses ABCD study data (n=4,641 ages 9-12) at baseline, Year1, Year2 follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity (SMA) as qualifying smartphone/screen time variable; B1 applies - CLPM reports separate coefficients for SMA, sleep duration, psychopathology

FT_IC3.evidence: Cross-Lagged Panel Models (CLPMs) investigating reciprocal predictive relationships; multiple regression coefficients reported with 95% CIs (e.g., β=-0.10 [95% CI: -0.16, -0.03])

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients with 95% CIs reported in abstract and results (β=-0.10, β=-0.11, β=0.11, β=0.07, β=0.09, indirect effect=0.032)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD study; investigation of temporal relationships and mediation mechanisms between SMA, sleep, and psychopathology

FT_IC6.evidence: Published full-length report in Journal of Behavioral Addictions (2024), no evidence of preprint/duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: SMA is not dependent variable in all analyses; paper examines SMA as predictor AND outcome (bidirectional/cross-lagged); B20 applies - bidirectional associations disqualify from EC7
notes: Bidirectional CLPM design with SMA as both predictor and outcome in different lagged relationships. Brain structural covariation included as additional predictor beyond demographics.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study participants (n=10,828 youth, 47.8% female); baseline mean age 9.9 years, follow-up 10.9 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time as qualifying variable (youth self-reported screen time for weekdays/weekend); B1 applies - separate coefficients reported for screen time (dichotomized >2 vs ≤2 hours/day)

FT_IC3.evidence: Longitudinal study with bidirectional associations examined; logistic regression reporting odds ratios (OR) with 95% confidence intervals

FT_IC4.evidence: Odds ratios (OR) with 95% CIs reported in results section (e.g., OR 0.6 [95% CI 0.4-0.8], OR 1.6 [95% CI 1.1-2.2])

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis of ABCD Study examining associations between sleep duration, screen time, and internalizing symptoms

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original article in Journal of Adolescent Health (2024), no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time and sleep are predictors of internalizing symptoms (outcome), not dependent variables; paper examines bidirectional associations (B20 applies); gender-stratified analyses
notes: Bidirectional associations examined; gender-stratified analyses showed mostly unidirectional associations (behavior preceding symptoms) in females only

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study data from baseline (2017-2018) and one-year follow-up (2018-2019); nationally representative U.S. youth sample (N=10,544, 48.9% female)

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying screen time variables: streaming, gaming, socializing; B1 applies - separate coefficients reported for each form of screen time in isotemporal substitution and CoDA models

FT_IC3.evidence: Sex-stratified regression models (isotemporal substitution and compositional data analysis) estimating associations between baseline behaviors and follow-up BMI z-score

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients (β) with 95% confidence intervals reported (e.g., β=-0.03 [-0.05, -0.002], β=-0.03 [-0.05, -0.01], β=-0.03 [-0.06, -0.01])

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis of ABCD Study examining associations between different forms of screen time and BMI z-score

FT_IC6.evidence: Published open access research article in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity (2024), no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time variables are predictors of BMI z-score (outcome), not dependent variables; paper includes non-demographic predictors (internalizing symptoms as covariate)
notes: Isotemporal substitution and compositional data analysis approaches; different results by sex; examined streaming, gaming, socializing separately
